answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: 3.0°C Hot House World Scenario – Current trajectory
answers.evidence: "3.0°C Hot House World Scenario – Current trajectory: A world where global warming reaches 3.0 degrees above preindustrial levels by 2100, due to no additional policies introduced to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. 2.0°C Disorderly Scenario – Delayed transition: A world where global warming is limited to 2.0 degrees above preindustrial levels, as policies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions are introduced after 2030. There is a rapid and concerted effort to reach net zero 2050 goals. 1.5°C Orderly Scenario – Net zero 2050: A world where global warming is successfully limited to 1.5 degrees above preindustrial levels, as ambitious goals and policies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions are immediately and effectively implemented."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.20-21; Appendix A, p.52-55
answers.notes: Three scenarios used. No additional scenarios explored, as explicitly stated: "No additional scenarios have been explored."

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.5°C (Orderly), 2.0°C (Disorderly), and >3.0°C (Hot House World). All are expressed as global average temperature increases above pre-industrial levels by 2100. No temperature overshoot is mentioned.
answers.evidence: "Note: Within scenario titles, the degrees Celsius (e.g. 1.5°C, 2.0°C, 3.0°C) refers to the global average temperature increase above pre-industrial levels. Regional and local temperature changes may vary from the global average." And: "3.0°C Hot House World Scenario – Current trajectory: A world where global warming reaches 3.0 degrees above preindustrial levels by 2100" / "2.0°C Disorderly Scenario – Delayed transition: A world where global warming is limited to 2.0 degrees above preindustrial levels" / "1.5°C Orderly Scenario – Net zero 2050: A world where global warming is successfully limited to 1.5 degrees above preindustrial levels"
answers.location_hint: Glossary p.46-47; Strategy p.20-21; Appendix A p.52-55
answers.notes: No temperature overshoot language found in the document.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are named: (1) The Aotearoa Circle (Tourism Sector Climate Scenarios); (2) Beca Limited for the New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC) (Construction and Property Sector Climate Scenarios); (3) XRB/NZ CS (mandated scenario requirements). The entity-level scenarios are MCK's own, drawn from and consistent with these sector scenarios.
answers.evidence: "MCK considered the work undertaken by the Aotearoa Circle to develop climate scenarios and adaptation strategies for the tourism sector as a whole. Given that MCK is an owner operator of hotels and has significant physical assets, we have also had regard to the sector scenarios developed by Beca Limited for Te Kaunihera Hanganga Tautaiao | New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC). These sector Orderly, Disorderly and Hot house world scenarios are based on a coherent set of socio-economic assumptions, decarbonisation pathways, and climate change projections."
answers.notes: The mandated NZ CS scenario requirements (from XRB) also shaped the scenario selection, as noted in footnote 17.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "SSP3-7.0 climate change projection scenario was used for this exposure assessment. This aligns to the Hot house scenario and of the five IPCC scenarios, it is one of the upper middle warming scenarios for which NIWA data is available."
answers.notes: Only one SSP identifier (SSP3-7.0) is mentioned, used for the physical exposure assessment. No RCP identifiers are mentioned anywhere in the document. There is no mixing of SSP and RCP families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "MCK's entity-level scenarios draw from, and are consistent with, the sector scenarios (but downscaled for our business) and therefore support comparability with the disclosures of others in our sector. Our scenarios reflect MCK's specific circumstances, being an amalgam of the scenarios outlined for both sectors." And: "Since our first disclosures we have reviewed the relevant tourism and property sector scenarios; reviewed MCK's prior scenario analysis against latest standards and guidance; and tailored entity-level scenarios to focus on climate drivers with material impact to our business."
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses the words "downscaled for our business" and "tailored entity-level scenarios", confirming customisation beyond off-the-shelf use.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "MCK undertook scenario analysis using internal staff resources and working groups led by the Vice President Legal and Sustainability Manager; support and peer review by external experts; and workshops with senior leadership, Hotel General Managers and Directors. This was to ensure that they are understood by the business and can be factored into decision making."
answers.notes: The scenario development involved internal staff, external expert peer review, and internal workshops, but no language of "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", or "collaborative development" with external stakeholders is used. The process is internal with external peer review only.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described. The Hot House World (3.0°C) scenario is described as "Current trajectory" and represents a world with no additional policies, but it is not formally designated as a baseline against which the other scenarios are compared.
answers.evidence: "3.0°C Hot House World Scenario – Current trajectory: A world where global warming reaches 3.0 degrees above preindustrial levels by 2100, due to no additional policies introduced to reduce greenhouse gas emissions."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.20-21; Appendix A, p.54-55
answers.notes: The Hot House scenario is labelled "Current trajectory" which implies it represents a no-additional-policy baseline, but the document does not explicitly frame it as a formal baseline or reference scenario against which the others are compared.

answers.question: Model used
answer: SSP3-7.0 (IPCC scenario used for physical exposure assessment via NIWA data)
answers.evidence: "SSP3-7.0 climate change projection scenario was used for this exposure assessment. This aligns to the Hot house scenario and of the five IPCC scenarios, it is one of the upper middle warming scenarios for which NIWA data is available." Also: "Sources include local government entities, the Institute of Geological and Nuclear Sciences (GNS), and the National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA)."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, footnote 20, p.22-23; Risk Management section, p.32-33
answers.notes: No named climate models (e.g. CLIMADA, REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM) or macroeconomic models are identified. SSP3-7.0 is an IPCC scenario identifier, not a model name per se. NIWA data is used for physical projections. The narrative scenarios are qualitative and do not reference specific IAMs.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "The exposure of MCK properties to geohazards, including landslides, flooding, sea level rise and coastal inundation and erosion was assessed, as well as 15 climate variables including temperature, rainfall and wind projections." And: "We have already taken some remedial action to reduce the vulnerability of our hotels at greatest risk to acute physical climate impacts (such as protection of electrical services; on site sandbags; and basement pumps to reduce flood impact)." And from the Hot House scenario: "More frequent extreme weather events cause significant disruption to travel and hotel occupancy and increased expense for preparedness and repairs."
answers.location_hint: Risk Management section, p.32-33; Strategy section, p.28-29; Appendix A, p.54-55
answers.notes: Acute physical risks (flooding, coastal inundation, landslides, extreme weather events) are explicitly assessed and included in the scenario analysis. However, damage/loss estimates are qualitative (risk ratings: no/low/moderate/high) rather than quantified financial losses.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: whole of life building emission reduction requirement
answers.evidence: "Key 2050 Indicators: Sea level rise, NZ native forestry, Number of hot days >25C, Extreme rainfall, Whole of life building emission reduction requirement, Carbon price, NZ net emissions." And: "Climate variables: Physical aspects of climate that exhibit a measurable change overtime including but not limited to, air temperature, number of very hot days, hottest day, solar radiation, coldest day, heavy rain, number of very rainy days, number of dry days, and strong wind." And: "The exposure of MCK properties to geohazards, including landslides, flooding, sea level rise and coastal inundation and erosion was assessed, as well as 15 climate variables including temperature, rainfall and wind projections."
answers.location_hint: Appendix A p.52-55; Glossary p.46-47; Risk Management p.32-33
answers.notes: Variables appear across both the narrative scenario indicators (2050 key indicators) and the physical risk assessment (15 climate variables). Carbon price is a key transition variable used across all three scenarios.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "SSP3-7.0 climate change projection scenario was used for this exposure assessment. This aligns to the Hot house scenario and of the five IPCC scenarios, it is one of the upper middle warming scenarios for which NIWA data is available." And: "Geospatial analysis was used to overlay property locations with climate-related hazard data sourced from locally relevant and available sources. Sources include local government entities, the Institute of Geological and Nuclear Sciences (GNS), and the National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA)." And: "MCK's entity-level scenarios draw from, and are consistent with, the sector scenarios (but downscaled for our business)"
answers.location_hint: Strategy section footnote 20, p.22-23; Risk Management p.32-33
answers.notes: The narrative scenarios are global in framing (referencing global warming targets) but the physical risk assessment uses locally-sourced NIWA data and geospatial analysis at the property level. The entity-level scenarios are described as "downscaled for our business."

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Two sets of time horizons are used: (1) For transition risks/opportunities: Short-term = Present–2030; Medium-term = 2031–2045; Long-term = 2046–2075. (2) For physical climate variables (aligned to MfE guidance): Short-term = 2021–2040; Medium-term = 2041–2060; Long-term = 2081–2100. Rationales are provided for both sets.
answers.evidence: "Our time horizons remain substantively the same as identified in our first disclosures. These are consistent with business planning, consider the longer-term nature of owned buildings and hotel refurbishment cycles, align to building depreciation, and they are largely consistent with sector scenarios. Noting that our assessment of physical climate variables across our portfolio has been aligned to time frames consistent with MfE guidance, i.e. short-term (2021–2040), medium-term (2041–2060) and long-term (2081–2100). MCK has used the following time horizons to inform scenario analysis and assess the climate-related transition risks and opportunities identified as short, medium or long-term. Present–2030 [Short-term]: Impacts on near-term income, operating costs and increased repairs and maintenance. 2031–2045 [Medium-term]: 7–10 year refurbishment cycle. Impacts on core aspects of strategy, acquisition of new hotels, expansion of the portfolio, development phasing and/or divestment decisions. 2046–2075 [Long-term]: 30–50 year life of primary property assets. Impacts from travel patterns, trading conditions and long-term viability."
answers.notes: Rationales are explicitly provided for each time horizon, linked to business planning cycles (refurbishment cycles, building depreciation, asset life). Physical risk time horizons are aligned to MfE guidance.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "Our Orderly and Hot House world scenarios, align with the mandated NZ CS scenarios. We selected our remaining Disorderly scenario, to represent a challenging future for our business as it is designed to explore a disrupted climate transition. No additional scenarios have been explored." Footnote 17: "(i) at least one 1.5°C, Paris Agreement-aligned scenario, (ii) a greater than 3°C-aligned high warming scenario, and (iii) a third scenario." And: "In developing scenarios, MCK had regard to two sector archetypes which it considers important for its operations and reflects the nature of our core business. MCK considered the work undertaken by the Aotearoa Circle to develop climate scenarios and adaptation strategies for the tourism sector as a whole. Given that MCK is an owner operator of hotels and has significant physical assets, we have also had regard to the sector scenarios developed by Beca Limited for Te Kaunihera Hanganga Tautaiao | New Zealand Green Building Council (NZGBC)."
answers.notes: Justification is provided on two grounds: (1) compliance with mandated NZ CS scenario requirements (Orderly and Hot House); (2) relevance to MCK's specific business context as both a tourism and property operator (Disorderly scenario chosen to explore disrupted transition). Sector-specific sources (Aotearoa Circle and NZGBC/Beca) are cited as the basis for scenario development.
